text: The video shows a direct hernia on the left side of a seventy-year-old male patient. The medial umbilical ligament is visible, along with the hernia sac, which measures approximately three centimeters. The surgeon identifies the triangle of doom and triangle of pain, and begins dissection starting from the medial umbilical ligament. The iliac vessel is visible with noticeable pulsation. The harmonic scalpel is used to dissect the medial umbilical ligament above the hernia arc.

The video shows a seventy-year-old male with a direct hernia on the left side. The medial umbilical ligament is visible, along with the hernia sac, which measures approximately three centimeters. The surgeon identifies the triangle of doom and triangle of pain, and begins dissection from the medial umbilical ligament. The iliac vessel is visible, showing pulsation. Using a harmonic device, the surgeon dissects the medial umbilical ligament. Cold scissors are used to gently cut the peritoneum and separate it from the transversalis fascia.

The surgeon uses cold scissors to gently cut the peritoneum and separate it from the transversalis fascia, creating a preperitoneal pocket. The scissors break fibrous tissue to facilitate separation, while a grasper holds the peritoneum, providing inferior medial traction to allow gas entry and aid in dissecting the preperitoneal space. The dissection extends up to six centimeters lateral to the outer margin of the deep ring towards the ten o'clock position, ensuring the mesh will cover the myopectineal orifice.

The surgeon uses scissors to carefully separate the fibrous tissue from the peritoneum, creating a preperitoneal pocket. The grasper holds the peritoneum, applying inferior medial traction to allow gas to enter and assist in dissecting the preperitoneal space. The dissection extends up to six centimeters lateral to the outer margin of the deep ring towards the ten o'clock position, ensuring the mesh will cover the myopectineal orifice.

During the inguinal hernia repair, the surgeon uses the back of the scissors as a spatula to separate the peritoneum in an inferior medial direction, aiding in the creation of the preperitoneal space.
The surgeon uses the back of the scissors as a spatula to separate the peritoneum in an inferior medial direction, aiding in the dissection of the preperitoneal space.

The surgeon begins the dissection using a commercially available placelet from Covidien for blunt dissection to separate the peritoneum. The dissection focuses on the triangle of pain, starting with lateral dissection followed by medial dissection to separate the hernia sac. The transversalis fascia is visible, and a pre-peritoneal pocket is created to accommodate a large mesh. The sigmoid colon and iliac vessel are identified, and careful dissection keeps the fat away from the peritoneum. The dissection is performed without energy devices, using the placelet to avoid bleeding.

The surgeon begins dissection using a commercially available Covidien placelet for blunt dissection to separate the peritoneum. The lateral dissection of the triangle of pain is carried out, revealing the transversalis fascia and creating a pre-peritoneal pocket. The sigmoid colon and iliac vessel are visible as the peritoneum is carefully dissected laterally.

The surgeon carefully dissects the peritoneum within the triangle of pain, keeping the preperitoneal fat away from the peritoneum. The dissection is performed using a placelet without any energy devices, resulting in no bleeding. The peritoneum is visibly separating from the underlying preperitoneal fat.

The surgeon completes the lateral dissection of the peritoneum within the triangle of pain, revealing the preperitoneal fat. The grasper stretches the peritoneum inferomedially, facilitating entry into the medial dissection. The Cooper's ligament becomes visible as the surgeon continues the dissection, separating the bladder from the retropubic space, known as the space of Retzius, using gentle blunt dissection.

The lateral dissection is nearly complete, and the surgeon proceeds with the medial dissection. The grasper stretches the peritoneum inferomedially, allowing entry into the medial dissection to expose the Cooper's ligament and the space of Retzius. The transversalis fascia is visible, and the Cooper's ligament begins to appear as the surgeon continues medially to separate the bladder from the retropubic space.
The surgeon performs gentle blunt dissection to separate the medial peritoneum, revealing the opaque transversalis fascia. The peritoneum is pushed downward, exposing the space of Retzius.

The surgeon identifies the opaque transversalis fascia and the space of Hesselbach's triangle. The Cooper's ligament, known for its pearly white color, is visible and reflects light. After completing lateral and medial dissections, the surgeon proceeds with sac dissection, carefully separating the peritoneum from the transversalis fascia. The peritoneum is pulled inferomedially while the transversalis fascia is pushed away, allowing the vas deferens and spermatic vessels to separate from the peritoneum.

The surgeon dissects medially away from the midline towards the right side, identifying the Cooper's ligament, which appears pearly white and reflects light. After completing lateral and medial dissections, the surgeon proceeds with sac dissection, using the boundary of the inferior epigastric vessel above. The peritoneum is pulled inferomedially while the transversalis fascia is pushed, allowing the vas deferens and spermatic vessels to separate from the peritoneum.

The surgeon carefully separates the pseudo-sac, known as the transversal fascia, from the real sac during the hernia procedure. The pseudo-sac is pushed away from the peritoneum, ensuring that the dissection remains close to the peritoneum to avoid bleeding. As the peritoneal dissection progresses, the peritoneum visibly sags downward.

The surgeon carefully separates the pseudo-sac from the peritoneum, ensuring proximity to the peritoneum to avoid bleeding. The peritoneum is sagging down, revealing the inverted sac of a direct hernia. The dissection extends to the triangle of doom, separating the peritoneum from the vas deferens and spermatic vessels. A preperitoneal pocket is created around the defect, with anatomical landmarks including the inferior epigastric vessel and iliac vessel visible. The dissection is nearly complete, allowing for mesh placement.

The surgeon inverts the hernia sac, revealing the direct hernia defect. The peritoneum is separated from the triangle of doom, with careful dissection around the vas deferens and spermatic vessels. A preparatory pocket is created, ensuring a healthy margin around the defect for mesh placement. The inferior epigastric vessel is identified above the triangle of doom, and the iliac vessel pulsation is checked during dissection.

The surgeon mobilizes the peritoneum, causing the sigmoid colon to drop down over the triangle of doom. The iliac vessel area is visible, and the space of Retzius is accessed for further medial dissection to create a pocket for mesh placement. The dissection is complete, with the sac removed and the defect visible lateral to the inferior epigastric vessel. The mesh is introduced to cover the myopectineal orifice, ensuring it does not fold or corrugate, and it spans the inferior epigastric vessel, vas deferens, spermatic vessel, triangle of doom, triangle of pain, trapezoid of disaster, and Cooper ligament.

The surgeon mobilizes the peritoneum, causing the sigmoid colon to drop down. The peritoneum is further separated over the triangle of doom, revealing the iliac vessel area. The space of Retzius is visible, and additional medial dissection is performed to create a pocket for mesh placement. The vas deferens and spermatic vessels are identified, indicating the dissection is nearly complete.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the pre-peritoneal space and removes the hernia sac. The defect is visible just lateral to the inferior epigastric vessel. A mesh is carefully introduced to cover the entire myopectineal orifice, ensuring it is fully spread without corrugation. The mesh covers the inferior epigastric vessel, vas deferens, spermatic vessel, triangle of doom, triangle of pain, trapezoid of disaster, and extends to the space of radius, also covering the Cooper ligament.

The surgeon ensures that the mesh is not folded at any corner and enlarges the space to cover the entire myopectineal orifice. The Cooper ligament is palpated for its hardness, and a titanium tacker is used to fix the mesh to the Cooper ligament. Additional tackers are placed over the transversus abdominis muscle and the rectus abdominis, avoiding the area below the inguinal ligament. The peritoneum is approximated to cover the mesh completely, using the double brass technique to prevent adhesion.

The surgeon ensures that the mesh is not folded at any corner and enlarges the space if necessary to cover the entire myopectineal orifice. The Cooper ligament is identified by its hardness, and the first titanium tacker is used to fix the mesh to the Cooper ligament. Additional tackers are applied over the transversus abdominis muscle and the rectus abdominis, while avoiding placement below the inguinal ligament, taking care to protect the inferior epigastric vessel.

The surgeon approximates the lower edge of the peritoneum, lifting it to cover the mesh completely using the double brass technique. The peritoneum is sutured to ensure the mesh is fully covered, preventing adhesion. As the gas is decreased, the peritoneum further reinforces the mesh, adhering to Pascal's principle of hydrostatic pressure.

The surgeon ensures the mesh is fully covered by the peritoneum, preventing any adhesions. As the gas pressure decreases, the peritoneum further exposes and reinforces the mesh, which is now completely covering the surgical area. The mesh is not visible, indicating successful coverage.
The peritoneum is fully covering the mesh, ensuring that the mesh is not visible. The surgical field is clear, indicating the completion of the procedure for an indirect left-sided inguinal hernia.
